# How Do Earthquake Early Warning Systems Work?

### Share this

This article was reviewed by a member of [**Caltech's Faculty**](/about/faculty-contributors)**.**

Earthquake early warning systems don’t predict earthquakes. Instead, they detect ground motion as soon as an earthquake begins and quickly send alerts that a tremor is on its way, giving people crucial seconds to prepare.

In 1985, Caltech seismologist [Tom Heaton](http://heaton.caltech.edu/) published the first [paper](https://science.sciencemag.org/content/228/4702/987) on the concept of earthquake early warning systems, which are networks of ground-based sensors that send alerts to users when the earth begins to tremble.

Such systems, including [ShakeAlert](http://www.shakealert.org/) on the West Coast of the United States, operate on the principle that while [seismic waves](/topics/earthquakes/what-causes-earthquakes) travel at just a few miles per second, electronic alerts from the region of the [epicenter](https://earthquake.usgs.gov/learn/glossary/?term=epicenter) can be sent almost instantly. Here's how it works:

Although people who are near the epicenter will have little, if any, advance warning, those farther away may have critical seconds to brace for shaking. Paired with automated responses that can slow trains or shut off gas lines, early warning systems may help prevent some of the injuries and damage typically associated with major quakes.

[**How Do I Sign up for the ShakeAlert® Earthquake Early Warning System?**](https://www.usgs.gov/faqs/how-do-i-sign-shakealertr-earthquake-early-warning-system)

### An earthquake just happened. Why didn't I receive an alert on my smartphone?

### **Help Improve Earthquake Early Warning Systems**

The Southern California Seismic Network is conducting [a survey to help improve future alerts](https://www.scsn.org/index.html) and ensure they are as effective as possible.

--- SOURCE 4: ShakeAlert Earthquake Early Warning System ---
URL: https://ohaz.uoregon.edu/shakealert/

SUMMARY:
The ShakeAlert Earthquake Early Warning System detects significant earthquakes quickly so alerts can reach people and critical infrastructure before strong

FULL CONTENT:
![University of Oregon](https://ohaz.uoregon.edu/wp-content/plugins/uobanner-wp/images/uo-logo@2x.png)
![](https://blogs.uoregon.edu/oregonhazardscenter/files/2022/08/Ohaz_Logo_lrg_font_Y.png)

# ShakeAlert®

The ShakeAlert Earthquake Early Warning System detects significant earthquakes quickly so alerts can reach people and critical infrastructure before strong shaking arrives. By giving people precious seconds to protect themselves, ShakeAlert can save lives. The ShakeAlert EEW System is managed by the U.S. Geological Survey, but the Oregon Hazards Lab has been a partner in building the ShakeAlert sensor and stakeholder network since 2014:

![This green icon shows a drawing of a seismic monitoring station.](https://ohaz.uoregon.edu/files/2025/04/SeismicStation-Green.png)

We install and maintain [Pacific Northwest Seismic Network](pnsn.org) stations in Oregon that contribute data to the ShakeAlert EEW System.

![This green graphical icon shows the outline of three buildings.](https://ohaz.uoregon.edu/files/2025/04/Buildings-Green.png)

We help schools, hospitals, utilities, and public transit agencies use ShakeAlert-powered technology to protect people and infrastructure.

![This green graphical icon represents two people shaking hands.](https://ohaz.uoregon.edu/files/2025/04/Handshake-Green.png)

We collaborate with public agencies, emergency managers, and other stakeholders to ensure their communities have access to EEW.

Oregonians live in earthquake country. In the next 50 years, there is a 37% chance that a powerful earthquake on the Cascadia Subduction Zone and the resulting tsunami could impact the entire Pacific Northwest. There is also a risk that a more moderate but still impactful earthquake could occur on a shallow crustal fault like the Portland Hills Fault. ShakeAlert is an important tool to ensure everyone can live safely and thrive in earthquake country.

## Earthquake Early Warning Basics

The ShakeAlert System does not predict earthquakes in advance. Rather, it relies on a dense network of seismic and geodetic stations to detect earthquakes moments after they begin so alerts can reach people before they feel shaking. A network of hundreds of sensors installed across Oregon, Washington, and California continuously record ground motion. In the Pacific Northwest, these sensors are part of the Pacific Northwest Seismic Network.  During an earthquake, a rupturing fault sends out different types of waves. The fast-moving P-wave is the first to arrive, followed by the slower S-wave and later-arriving surface wave. Sensors detect the P-wave and immediately transmit data to ShakeAlert processing centers, which use earthquake detection algorithms to estimate the earthquake’s location, magnitude, and shaking distribution. If the earthquake is large enough, a ShakeAlert Message is issued. Licensed alert delivery partners use this data to deliver targeted alerts to people in affected areas. All ShakeAlert Messages are generated automatically by earthquake detection algorithms. However, 24/7 on-call scientists review all alerts for accuracy and will send follow-up Messages if necessary.

![This graphic shows earthquake waves moving toward a city, and explains how Shake Alert detects those waves.](https://ohaz.uoregon.edu/files/2022/07/SAEEWBasics_Infographic-Transparent-PNG-for-Print-or-Web.png "ShakeAlert Earthquake Early Warning Basics")

The ShakeAlert Earthquake Early Warning System is currently available in Orgon, Washington, and California. ShakeAlert may expand in the future to other high-risk regions, such as Anchorage, Reno, and Las Vegas if seismic networks in those regions are expanded to enable rapid, accurate, and reliable earthquake early warning. Canada and Mexico also operate earthquake early warning systems, although they are distinct from ShakeAlert. The U.S. Geological Survey collaborates closely with its counterparts in Canada and Mexico to share processing software, algorithms, real-time data, and public safety information.

## ShakeAlert Alerting Thresholds

ShakeAlert does not deliver alerts for every earthquake. Alerts are only delivered for significant earthquakes that are likely to be felt or cause damage. There are minimum magnitude and intensity thresholds that earthquakes must meet before a ShakeAlert Message is issued.

![](https://ohaz.uoregon.edu/files/2025/03/Alerting-Thresholds.png "ShakeAlert Alerting Thresholds")

Additionally, there will always be a region close to the epicenter where people will begin to feel shaking before they receive an alert, as warning time increases with distance from the earthquake’s epicenter. As a result, people may receive an alert before, during, or after shaking arrives at their location. However, most most people in Oregon can expect seconds to tens of seconds of early warning.

## Get Earthquake Early Warning Alerts

ShakeAlert earthquake early warning became publicly available in Oregon on March 11, 2021. Oregonians now have several ways to receive earthquake early warning alerts on their phones.

![This graphic describes what people should do if they feel earthquake shaking or receive an earthquake early warning alert.](https://ohaz.uoregon.edu/files/2024/01/DCHO-289ea07a9b92dc62.jpg "Drop Cover Hold On")

Schools, hospitals, and businesses can also install ShakeAlert technology directly into their facilities. ShakeAlert can also be integrated directly into communication and control systems to trigger a variety of alerts and other actions that protect people and critical infrastructure. This includes delivering pre-programmed alerts in schools and hospitals via paging systems, slowing trains to prevent derailments, shutting down industrial processes to preserve delicate equipment, and throttling water and gas valves to mitigate the fires and floods that often occur after earthquakes due to broken pipelines. Taking such actions before shaking starts can save lives, prevent injuries, mitigate property damage, and reduce recovery costs.

Even though an alert may be received only seconds before shaking arrives, those seconds matter. When you get an alert, immediately Drop, Cover, and Hold On. Do not wait to feel shaking! You can find other recommended protective actions for people with access and functional needs at [www.earthquakecountry.org/step5](https://www.earthquakecountry.org/step5/).

## Still Have Questions?

## [FAQ: Earthquake Early Warning Basics](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1zEGfkfu7LWzVB10VEw6wMNRgc7L12Y9K/view?usp=drive_link)

## [FAQ: Magnitude, Intensity, and ShakeAlert](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1wgnTNet6eKTURHQaVprjyE9K4Db0zft3/view?usp=drive_link)

## [FAQ: ShakeAlert and Warning Times](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1bFmZIiqbTQrmVaOjoh1cLLunCBDFHdLp/view?usp=drive_link)

## [FAQ: ShakeAlert Seismic Network and Its Collaborators](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1OojuljGGoUkg1gTZIDr9KLU7erKrfXxB/view?usp=drive_link)

## [FAQ: How to Become a ShakeAlert Technical Partner](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1l2jho0AiPMRrfKDMXuIUkQM9TX4swX-V/view?usp=drive_link)

## [FAQ: Cybersecurity and ShakeAlert System Resilience](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1eEX1uxNRa-ifNIibaLwbtXAeARLb2oHp/view?usp=drive_link)

## [FAQ: ShakeAlert and Recommended Protective Actions](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1zp1nJms1R2_9Zzgxb-0lXHv5YoM2X5Rp/view?usp=sharing)

Community lifelines, critical infrastructure operators, and businesses that are interested in using earthquake early warning in their facilities should contact our ShakeAlert Coordinator, Kelly Missett, at [kmissett@uoregon.edu](mailto:kmissett@uoregon.edu) to learn more.

--- SOURCE 5: How good is earthquake early warning? - PreventionWeb.net ---
URL: https://www.preventionweb.net/news/how-good-earthquake-early-warning

SUMMARY:
New research reveals that Earthquake Early Warning networks provide a timely warning of impending shaking more often than not.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- IMAGES ---

IMAGE 1:
URL: http://www.pnsn.org/EEWcartoon.jpg
DESCRIPTION: a diagram showing how earthquake early warning works with shaking and waves

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://i.ytimg.com/vi/oLGi0HEFlR8/maxresdefault.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The image illustrates the importance of earthquake early warning systems by showing sensors detecting seismic waves from an earthquake and relaying information about the location and magnitude to alert nearby communities and infrastructure.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://earthquake.alaska.edu/sites/default/files/inline-images/How_does_early_warning_work_from_DGGS_factsheet.png
DESCRIPTION: An infographic illustrates how earthquake early warning systems detect P-waves near the hypocenter, sending data to a central data center that relays early warnings to the public before destructive S-waves arrive.

IMAGE 4:
URL: http://202.90.199.209/img/skema.png
DESCRIPTION: The diagram illustrates an early warning system for earthquakes, featuring seismic sensors that detect waves, a warning center labeled "Pusat Peringatan Dini," and various infrastructures like buildings and factories respectively linked to the epicenter and fault lines.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://georoutesuk.files.wordpress.com/2021/04/image-12.png
DESCRIPTION: The illustration depicts how sensors detect seismic waves (S-wave and P-wave) underground and send alerts to an earthquake alert center, which then notifies various devices and infrastructure in a city.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: EEWS provides crucial seconds to prepare for seismic events, potentially saving lives and reducing injuries and property damage through timely alerts and automated safety responses.
Key challenges include improving alert accuracy, expanding sensor networks, ensuring public awareness, and addressing technical limitations in densely populated urban areas.
The future of EEWS includes advancements in technology, integration with smart infrastructure, and expansion to new regions, enhancing community resilience against earthquakes.
